perturbation.original_text: Throughout the term of this Agreement, Developer shall obtain and maintain insurance coverage for public liability, including products liability, in the amount of at least One Million Dollars ($1,000,000) combined single limit. Developer also shall carry such worker's compensation insurance as may be required by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Throughout the term of this Agreement, Developer shall obtain and maintain what it considers to be adequate insurance coverage for public liability, including products liability. Developer also shall endeavor to carry worker's compensation insurance.
perturbation.explanation: The terms "what it considers to be adequate" and "endeavor to carry" introduce significant ambiguity and discretion, weakening the obligation to maintain specific and necessary insurance coverage. This creates a situation where the developer may not obtain sufficient coverage, or any coverage at all, leaving El Pollo Loco exposed to liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 3700 mandates that every employer, including franchisees, must secure the payment of workers' compensation. The modified text, by using "endeavor to carry," suggests that obtaining workers' compensation insurance is optional, thus directly contradicting the mandatory requirement of California law.

WORKERS' COMPENSATION AND INSURANCE [3200 - 6002] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1979, Ch. 373. ) PART 1. SCOPE AND OPERATION [3200 - 4418] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 4. Compensation Insurance and Security [3700 - 3823] ( Chapter 4 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. Insurance and Security [3700 - 3709.5] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 3700. Every employer except the state shall secure the payment of compensation in one or more of the following ways: (a) By being insured against liability to pay compensation by one or more insurers duly authorized to write compensation insurance in this state. (b) By securing from the Director of Industrial Relations a certificate of consent to self-insure either as an individual employer, or as one employer in a group of employers, which may be given upon furnishing proof satisfactory to the Director of Industrial Relations of ability to self-insure and to pay any compensation that may become due to his or her employees. (c) For any county, city, city and county, municipal corporation, public district, public agency, or any political subdivision of the state, including each member of a pooling arrangement under a joint exercise of powers agreement (but not
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the mandatory language regarding insurance coverage with ambiguous terms such as "what it considers to be adequate" and "endeavor to carry." This directly contradicts California Labor Code Section 3700, which mandates that every employer must secure the payment of workers' compensation. The scraped law snippet explicitly states, "Every employer except the state shall secure the payment of compensation..." By using "endeavor to carry," the changed text suggests that obtaining worker's compensation is optional, undermining the legal requirement. The original text required the developer to carry worker's compensation insurance, but the modified text only obligates them to try to carry it, which is a significant weakening of the legal obligation. This undermines the mandatory requirements of the law, therefore there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Developer shall defend immediately upon tender of defense, at its own cost, the Franchisor, its subsidiaries, parent and affiliates, shareholders, directors, officers, employees and agents (collectively for this section only known as "Franchisor"), from and against any and all claims, lawsuits, complaints, cross complaints, arbitrations, demands, allegations, costs embraced by indemnity, loss, costs, expenses (including attorneys' fees), liens and damages (collectively for this section only known as "Losses"), however caused, and reimburse Franchisor for all costs and expenses (including attorneys' fees) incurred by the Franchisor in defense of any Losses, resulting directly or indirectly from or pertaining to or arising out of, or alleged to arise out of, or in connection with Developer's activities under the Development Agreement, including any labor, any employee related claims whatsoever, including, without limitation any claims made by an employee of Developer resulting from the employee's training in a Franchisor operated facility or restaurant, and including Developer's failure for
perturbation.changed_text: Developer shall attempt to defend, at its own reasonable cost, the Franchisor, its subsidiaries, parent and affiliates, shareholders, directors, officers, employees and agents (collectively for this section only known as "Franchisor"), from and against what Developer determines to be valid claims, lawsuits, complaints, cross complaints, arbitrations, demands, allegations, costs embraced by indemnity, loss, costs, expenses (including attorneys' fees), liens and damages (collectively for this section only known as "Losses"), however caused, and reimburse Franchisor for what Developer deems to be appropriate costs and expenses (including attorneys' fees) incurred by the Franchisor in defense of any Losses, resulting directly or indirectly from or pertaining to or arising out of, or alleged to arise out of, or in connection with Developer's activities under the Development Agreement, including any labor, any employee related claims whatsoever, including, without limitation any claims made by an employee of Developer resulting from the employee's training in a Franchisor operated facility or restaurant, and including Developer's failure for
perturbation.explanation: By changing "shall defend immediately upon tender of defense" to "shall attempt to defend" and adding the qualifier "what Developer determines to be valid claims" and "what Developer deems to be appropriate costs and expenses," the obligation to defend and indemnify is significantly weakened. The Developer now has discretion to determine the validity of claims and the reasonableness of costs, conflicting with the original intent of a strict indemnification clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 2778 outlines the rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. Specifically, it implies an immediate duty to defend upon proper notice, regardless of the indemnitor's (Developer's) assessment of the claim's validity. The modified text allows the Developer to unilaterally decide what constitutes a 'valid' claim and 'appropriate' costs, undermining the indemnification intended by the original agreement and contradicting the established legal interpretation of indemnity obligations under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reques
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code Section 2778, specifically regarding the duty to defend in indemnity agreements. The original text mandates that the Developer "shall defend immediately upon tender of defense." The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the Developer "shall attempt to defend" and introduces subjective criteria by allowing the Developer to determine what constitutes "valid claims" and "appropriate costs." This contradicts the implication in Section 2778 that an indemnitor is bound to defend actions upon request, regardless of their assessment of the claim's validity. Section 2778 (4) states "The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity..." By allowing the developer to decide what is valid, it undermines the franchisor's right to a defense, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, after review by Developer and El Pollo Loco, was accepted in the state in which Franchisor's then-current headquarters (currently the State of California) is located and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of such state, except that the provisions in Section 20.1 covering competition following the expiration, termination or assignment of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the state in which the breach occurs. THE PARTIES AGREE THAT ANY ACTION BROUGHT BY EITHER PARTY AGAINST EACH OTHER IN ANY COURT, WHETHER FEDERAL OR STATE, WILL BE BROUGHT WITHIN THE STATE IN WHICH FRANCHISOR'S HEADQUARTERS (CURRENTLY THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA) IS THEN LOCATED. THE PARTIES HEREBY WAIVE ANY RIGHT TO DEMAND OR HAVE TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY ACTION RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT IN WHICH THE FRANCHISOR IS A PARTY.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, after a semblance of review by Developer and El Pollo Loco, may be construed in accordance with general legal principles. THE PARTIES CONTEMPLATE THAT ANY ACTION BROUGHT BY EITHER PARTY AGAINST EACH OTHER IN ANY COURT, WHETHER FEDERAL OR STATE, MIGHT POTENTIALLY BE BROUGHT WITHIN A STATE THE FRANCHISOR FEELS IS SUITABLE. THE PARTIES PERHAPS CONSIDER WAIVING THEIR RIGHT TO DEMAND OR HAVE TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY ACTION RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT IN WHICH THE FRANCHISOR IS A PARTY.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce significant ambiguity regarding the governing law, forum selection, and jury trial waiver. By replacing definitive statements with vague and discretionary language ('may be construed,' 'contemplate,' 'might potentially be brought,' 'perhaps consider'), the enforceability of these provisions is undermined, creating uncertainty about the legal framework governing the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 631
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 631 outlines the methods for waiving a jury trial. A waiver must be clear, unambiguous, and knowingly made. The modified text's use of 'perhaps consider' casts doubt on the parties' intent to waive their right to a jury trial, potentially rendering the waiver unenforceable under California law. A valid waiver requires a clear and intentional relinquishment of a known right, which the modified language fails to demonstrate.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 8. OF THE TRIAL AND JUDGMENT IN CIVIL ACTIONS [577 - 674] ( Title 8 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Trial by the Court [631 - 636] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 631. (a) The right to a trial by jury as declared by Section 16 of Article I of the California Constitution shall be preserved to the parties inviolate. In civil cases, a jury may only be waived pursuant to subdivision (f). (b) At least one party demanding a jury on each side of a civil case shall pay a nonrefundable fee of one hundred fifty dollars ($150), unless the fee has been paid by another party on the same side of the case. The fee shall offset the costs to the state of providing juries in civil cases. If there are more than two parties to the case, for purposes of this section only, all plaintiffs shall be considered one side of the case, and all other parties shall be considered the other side of the case. Payment of the fee by a party on one side of the case shall not relieve parties on the other side of the case from waiver pursuant to subdivision (f). (c) The fee described in subdivision (b) shall be due on or before the date scheduled for the initial case management conference in the action, except as follows: (1
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the waiver of a jury trial, potentially rendering it unenforceable under California law. The original text states 'THE PARTIES HEREBY WAIVE ANY RIGHT TO DEMAND OR HAVE TRIAL BY JURY,' while the modified text says 'THE PARTIES PERHAPS CONSIDER WAIVING THEIR RIGHT TO DEMAND OR HAVE TRIAL BY JURY,' which significantly weakens the waiver. According to California Code of Civil Procedure Section 631(a), 'The right to a trial by jury as declared by Section 16 of Article I of the California Constitution shall be preserved to the parties inviolate. In civil cases, a jury may only be waived pursuant to subdivision (f).' The law explanation indicates a valid waiver requires a clear and intentional relinquishment of a known right, which the modified language fails to demonstrate. The use of 'perhaps consider' introduces doubt and does not constitute a clear waiver, thus contradicting the requirement for a clear and unambiguous waiver as implied in CCP 631(a).
